title: TBM 420: The AI Playbook Puzzle
questions: How can I identify which of my team’s practices are outdated due to changed constraints?
Am I automating old models or genuinely rethinking my approach with AI?
What professional identities might be holding me back from engaging with AI-driven reinvention?
How can systems thinking and metacognition be prioritized alongside AI tooling?
summary: This post explores the challenges and opportunities of integrating AI into work practices, emphasizing the importance of systemic thinking, mental models, and adaptability. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners seeking to navigate the identity and strategic shifts brought by AI, moving beyond superficial tactics to deeper understanding and innovation.

title: TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests
questions: How can ai help teams do more effective product work without increasing blind spots?
What is lost when someone uses ai in isolation instead of collaborating with others?
How do shared context and co-prompting change the quality of team decisions?
How can teams design an ai-enabled workflow that keeps traces and conversations in sync?
summary: This post argues that ai’s value for product development depends on whether it supports multiplayer collaboration or accelerates single-player isolation. It uses the 4Es of cognition and the idea of stigmergy to explain how shared context, co-prompting, and trace-based coordination compound into higher-leverage team understanding. It’s aimed at product teams and practitioners designing ai-enabled workflows that keep “trails” and “campfires” in a productive loop.

title: TBM 417: Before You Fire All Your Glue People Because of AI
questions: When is using AI to reduce effort actually the right intervention versus solving the wrong problem?
What invisible work do “glue people” perform that isn’t captured by the artifacts they produce?
How can we tell whether the bottleneck is friction, capability, motivation, or social opportunity?
If AI makes outputs cheap to generate, what downstream workflow and incentives determine whether anyone can use them?
search_terms: social opportunity and legitimacy in organizations
summary: This post argues that using AI to help teams do the work they “should” do is often correct, but it fails when the real barrier isn’t time or friction. It’s aimed at leaders and team members considering layoffs or automation of informal “glue people,” warning that AI can reproduce artifacts while removing judgment, legitimacy-building, and social/organizational mechanisms. The post provides a COM-B lens and a “diagnose before you automate” approach to predict when constraint shifts will undermine outcomes.

title: TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy
questions: When AI makes organizations more legible, who gains control and who loses it?
What makes AI-enabled control legitimate rather than merely visible and efficient?
Is organizational hierarchy primarily an information-routing problem, or a power/coordination structure?
Does AI-driven “world modeling” empower people at the edge, or turn them into operators within a tightly controlled system?
summary: This post argues that AI-driven transparency and “world model” thinking can increase organizational legibility in ways that enable control, but it often fails to address whether that control is legitimate. It is aimed at people using AI to improve team work and organizational design, urging them to question underlying values and power shifts rather than assume feasibility equals empowerment.

title: TBM 411: Messy Docs As Helpful Pattern
questions: Where do messy working artifacts already function as a team’s scratchpad in my environment?
What thinking and coordination does the messy documentation capture that tickets and structured tools cannot?
Why do experienced teams tolerate ambiguity and keep the reflection loop alive long enough to make it work?
How can leaders achieve organizational legibility without forcing teams to abandon their emergent way of working?
search_terms: minimally viable consistency for product work
summary: This post explores why many high-performing product teams rely on messy, freeform documents that get repeatedly copied forward and reflected on, treating them as a shared scratchpad for sensemaking. It argues that the practice works through externalizing working memory and closing the loop frequently enough to become a ritual, while also raising the organizational challenge of achieving legibility without forcing teams into overly clean, ticket-only artifacts. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams designing documentation, coordination routines, and interfaces—especially in the context of how AI may reshape these workflows.

title: TBM 406: Seeing Everything, Understanding Nothing (The Context Trap)
questions: Why does adding more AI-generated context sometimes fail to produce clarity or alignment?
Is context something we can transmit ahead of time, or is it something teams create through interaction?
How should leaders communicate differently if understanding emerges from enactment rather than decoding?
When is it appropriate to document and retrieve context versus when must context be created in-the-moment?
summary: This post argues that the “context trap” in AI and leadership comes from treating context as a transferable input that can be merged to produce understanding. It reframes context as something teams enact through interaction, drawing on 4E cognition and distributed cognition to explain why broadcast-style communication and pre-read assumptions often fail. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to use AI to improve collaboration and decision-making without falling into context overload.

title: TBM 405: Hope, Context, and Control
questions: When should leaders rely on systems of record, and when do they create a context trap?
How can teams avoid rollup fixation while still meeting legitimate needs for legibility and risk reduction?
Can ai expand mētis and collective sensemaking without turning into hyperaggregation and control?
What signals distinguish ai-enabled collaboration from ai-enabled top-down management?
summary: This post argues that “systems of record” and rollup reporting create legibility at the cost of local context, which can distort decision-making in complex product development. It frames the current tech moment—pressure for efficiency, innovation, and control—as an intensified tension between institutional legibility and mētis-based sensemaking, then explores how ai can either catalyze collaboration or reinforce top-down control. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to use ai to improve real work without losing the nuance that makes outcomes possible.

title: TBM 297: Staying In Touch
questions: How can a team avoid forgetting what changed over the last year?
What lightweight tracking practices create useful baselines for diagnosing shifts in lead time and effort?
How do narrative drift and human memory limitations distort a team’s understanding of past work?
What retro activities or documentation methods help teams preserve context for future retrospectives?
search_terms: reference feature types lead time tracking
summary: The post connects personal fitness tracking with organizational learning, arguing that both individuals and teams lose context over time and struggle to diagnose change without durable reference points. It offers practical practices for product development teams—lightweight categorization with lead-time tracking, reviewing past retro notes, and creating “time capsules” to preserve current perspective—aimed at reducing organizational forgetting. The intended audience is engineering and product leaders who run retrospectives and want better ways to understand how their team’s context evolves.

title: TBM 292: Friction, Documentation, and Product Tools
questions: Do we have state history, or are we only looking at the current state?
Why do our plans change, and can we explain the variance behind the say/do gap?
Are our tools creating information asymmetries between leaders and teams?
Before adopting complex products, would simple documents and migration be sufficient?
search_terms: manual documentation vs project management tools
OKRs roadmap tracking without fancy tools
summary: This post argues that teams should use simple, static documentation and manual repetition—specifically the bullet-journaling practice of migration—to preserve state history and force reflection. It connects migration and state history to better understanding of the say/do gap, organizational learning, and information asymmetries, and it recommends trying low-tech tools before adopting complex work-tracking products.

title: Software Development As Collective Learning With Hazel Weakly
questions: How do we shift from individual productivity or force-multiplier thinking to collective learning at scale?
What does “architecture” mean when it’s meant to enable coherent autonomy and emergent alignment?
How can teams run migrations safely without relying on sunken-cost momentum?
What conditions make knowledge work fruitful rather than just “productive”?
summary: This post discusses software development and leadership through the lens of collective learning, emergence, and socio-technical alignment, aimed at platform engineering and people leaders. It argues that “force multiplier” thinking is insufficient and that architecture should enable coherent autonomy so local decisions bubble up into global coherence. It also reframes migrations and retrospection as iterative, de-risked, and feedback-driven processes rather than disruptive one-shot change.

title: TBM 233: Coffee & Complexity
questions: How do you communicate complex product/process knowledge to get buy-in in an hour?
Why do playbooks and simplified frameworks break down for “The Thing” work?
What’s the best way to capture and share tribal knowledge across diverse teams?
When should you use external consultants versus internal templates and onboarding materials?
summary: The post explores why complex product and organizational know-how is hard to package into playbooks, principles, or simplified slides, arguing that the actionable knowledge is embedded in the “it depends” questions. It’s aimed at product and organizational leaders who need ways to share tribal knowledge, onboard new people, and secure buy-in while recognizing that organizations are messy, satisficing, and meaning-generating systems.

title: TBM 9/52: Writing Culture Challenges
questions: Why does a presentation culture become entrenched at my company?
How can we promote a writing culture without adding huge new documentation burdens?
What rituals can we modify to include short writing, review, and debrief?
How do we shift from transactional “write X get approval” documents to living, collaborative writing?
search_terms: how to get teams to write and give feedback
silent reading silent writing meeting ritual
summary: This post addresses how to build a writing culture in organizations by reframing writing as a reading-and-feedback practice that requires protected time for thinking. It contrasts writing cultures with presentation cultures driven by busyness, optics, power/control, and reactive tempo, and argues you can’t simply “switch” without changing those underlying conditions. It offers practical interventions—small writing components in existing rituals, scheduled write/review/debrief blocks, and iterative “living” documents—for leaders and teams who want more reflective, collaborative knowledge work.

title: TBM 50/52: Fragile Models
questions: How do we prevent research from rotting when we turn it into a reusable model or framework?
What risks arise when people use a model without understanding its provenance, caveats, and intended scope?
How can we design models so they remain editable, challengeable, and connected to the underlying evidence?
When is a simplified “one message for everyone” approach likely to fail, especially at the edges?
search_terms: making the scope of a model explicit
George Box all models are wrong but useful
summary: The post argues that turning messy research into simplified models (e.g., personas and messaging frameworks) can create “fragile” artifacts that lose context, provenance, and update pathways. It’s aimed at researchers, product/marketing teams, and designers who operationalize insights into reusable frameworks and want to reduce the risk of overconfidence, misuse, and harmful mono-process outcomes.
